story:
Mom kept asking if I was excited for my birthday, her smile bright but worried. She said everything would be all right, over and over, like she needed to hear it herself. I tried to match her cheer. Most of my friends had already done it. Dr. Hall said the procedure was pretty safe. My parents had saved for it since the day I was born. I told her I was ready. But the word “pretty” hung in the air.

Grandpa wasn’t my real grandpa. He was my grandpa’s grandpa—one of the first to benefit when medicine got good at replacing organs cheaply and well. People could stretch their lives to around 300, which satisfied most, until a brilliant AR/VR engineer-turned-doctor pushed for something else: immortality through virtual reality. The idea was simple and wild. Remove the brain, keep it in perfect chemical suspension, and connect it to the VR-verse. Inside, you could live forever, feel everything, go anywhere. You couldn’t tell the difference from the real world. The catch: once you went in, there was no going back.

On the day of my party, Grandpa found me on the deck, pacing. “Are you really doing it tomorrow?” he asked. He said I could wait until my two hundreds. There was no rush. I told him Mom was scared something might happen to me, like what happened to Tommy. He winced at my brother’s name. “What’s the difference if I live in the real universe or the VR-verse?” I asked. “They say it’s the same.” “And what if it isn’t?” he said. “No one can return to tell us. Your parents won’t see you again. They won’t know who you become.” I said Mom claimed she wasn’t pushing me, but her fear said otherwise. Grandpa nodded. She pushed because she was afraid of accidents—that was the point. Before we could go deeper, Dad stepped out and pulled me inside. Mom wasn’t even cutting cake anymore. She was talking to Galeb’s mom. Galeb had gone through with it a month earlier. As she left, Galeb’s mom hugged me hard and whispered, “Good luck.” I hugged her back.

It didn’t take much after that. That night, as I was heading to bed, I told Mom I’d decided to wait. I said I was too young to make this choice. Her face went white. “Are you sure?” she asked. I nodded. Before I could explain, my legs buckled. My words came out slurred. I heard Grandpa shouting, and Mom yelling back, panicked: she had given me the cocktail earlier, just like Dr. Hall had instructed, and he’d told her not to tell me so I wouldn’t be stressed. It was supposed to sedate me overnight so I’d sleep through the procedure. They tried to call the hospital to stop it. No one answered at first. By the time they finally got through, the transport team Dr. Hall had scheduled was already at the door, and everything after that dissolved into voices and hands and motion. The room slid away.

I woke up in the hospital. Everything seemed normal. I decided they must have reversed whatever the cocktail started. Dr. Hall came in first. He smiled at me. “So, everything went well, doctor?” I asked. “Yes, Jake,” he said, smile steady. “Just as we planned.”

I lay there, hearing the words. Whose plan? Mom’s and Dr. Hall’s? Mine? The world felt the same. That was the promise. That was the risk.
